coredata.dc:title: Co-doped N-rich carbon dots with enhanced peroxidase-like activity for dual-mode detection of tannic acid
coredata.prism:publicationName: Colloids and Surfaces A: Physicochemical and Engineering Aspects
coredata.prism:copyright: © 2025 Elsevier B.V. All rights are reserved, including those for text and data mining, AI training, and similar technologies.
coredata.prism:publisher: Elsevier B.V. All rights are reserved, including those for text and data mining, AI training, and similar technologies.
coredata.dc:creator: Song, Xiaoqing
Wen, Xiaoye
Huang, Yongfei
Fan, Zhefeng
coredata.dc:description: Sensitive and on-site detection of tannic acid (TA) was important for protecting human health. In this work, Co-doped N-rich carbon dots (Co-N-CDs) were synthesized using the vitamin B12, anhydrous citric acid and ethylenediamine as the precursors. The Co-N-CDs possessed high quantum yield and excellent peroxidase-like activity. The Michaelis constant (Km) of Co-N-CDs using H2O2 as substrate was lower 18.8 times than that of horseradish peroxidase (HRP). Compared to Co-doped carbon dots without ethylenediamine, the maximum reaction rate (Vmax) of Co-N-CDs toward 3,3′,5,5′-tetramethylbenzidine (TMB) was greatly enhanced around 5 times. In addition, the Co-N-CDs could oxidize TMB to oxidative product (oxTMB) in the presence of H2O2. The fluorescence intensity decreased due to the fluorescence resonance energy transfer (FRET) between the oxTMB and Co-N-CDs. When adding TA, the produced oxTMB was reduced, resulting in the absorbance decrease and fluorescence recovery. Based on the principle, a colorimetric and fluorometric dual-mode sensor based on Co-N-CDs nanozyme was constructed to detect TA. The limit of detection for fluorimetry and colorimetry were 0.028 μM and 0.041 μM, respectively. Meanwhile, a smartphone-assisted dual-mode sensing method was constructed to accomplish the portable and visual determination of TA in real samples with satisfactory recoveries, suggesting significant potential in food safety and environmental protection.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Co-doped N-rich carbon dots
Peroxidase-like nanozyme
Tannic acid
Dual-mode sensor

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Peroxidase-mimic catalytic property of the Co-N-CDs
Measurement of kinetic parameters of Co-N-CDs
Colorimetric and fluorescence detection of TA
Peroxidase-mimic catalytic feature of Co-N-CDs and optimization of reaction conditions
Smartphone-assisted dual-mode sensor for TA detection in actual samples

xocs:cp-notices.xocs:cp-notice: © 2025 Elsevier B.V. All rights are reserved, including those for text and data mining, AI training, and similar technologies.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (A) TEM, HR-TEM image (inset) and the size distribution histogram (inset) of the Co-N-CDs. (B) XRD pattern and (C) FT-IR spectrum of Co-N-CDs. (D) XPS spectrum of the Co-N-CDs and CDs.
(A) C1s XPS spectra (B) N1s XPS spectra (C) O1s XPS spectra and (D) Co 2p XPS spectra of Co-N-CDs.
(A) UV–vis spectrum, fluorescence excitation and emission spectra of Co-N-CDs. (B) Fluorescence emission spectra of Co-N-CDs under different excitation signals (290–370 nm).
Optimization of synthesis conditions (A) content of VB12, (B) volume of ethylenediamine.
Michaelis-Menten curves of Co-N-CDs by changing the concentrations of H2O2 (A) and TMB (B). The Lineweaver-Burk plots of Co-N-CDs by changing the concentrations of H2O2 (C) and TMB (D).

The Lineweaver-Burk plots of peroxidase-mimic activity of Co-N-CDs with different concentrations of H2O2 (A) and TMB (B). (C) Radical inhibition assays for TMB + H2O2 + Co-N-CDs systems. (D) EPR spectrum for •O2− identification. (E) EPR spectrum for 1O2 identification. (F) Peroxidase-mimic catalytic mechanism.
(A) UV–vis absorption spectra of oxTMB and excitation and emission spectra of Co-N-CDs. (B) The fluorescence lifetimes of Co-N-CDs and Co-N-CDs + oxTMB.

(A) UV–vis absorption spectra of probe for TA detection. (B) linear correlation plot of ΔA versus TA. (C) Fluorescence spectra of probe for TA detection. (D) linear correlation plot of F/F0 versus TA.

Selectivity and anti-interference assays of the Co-N-CDs for TA: (A) colorimetry (B) fluorimetry. The concentrations of TA, GA, and ascorbic acid were 3 μM, while the concentrations of other distractors were 9 μM. (C) Peroxidase-like activity of Co-N-CDs after 1 month.

(A) Schematic diagram of using the smartphone-assisted dual-mode sensor to detect TA. Colorimetric (B) and fluorescent (C) images under different concentrations of TA. (D) linear correlation plot for the colorimetric method. (E) linear correlation plot of I/I0 versus TA for the fluorescent mode.
Schematic diagram of dual-mode sensing platform for the detection of TA.
Determination of TA in actual samples.

head.ce:title: Co-doped N-rich carbon dots with enhanced peroxidase-like activity for dual-mode detection of tannic acid

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: School of Chemistry and Materials Science, Shanxi Normal University, Taiyuan 030031, PR China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: School of Chemistry and Materials Science, Shanxi Normal University

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: School of Chemistry and Material Science, Shanxi Normal University, Taiyuan, 030031, People’s Republic of China

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: College of Food Science, Shanxi Normal University, Taiyuan 030031, PR China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: College of Food Science, Shanxi Normal University

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: School of Food Science, Shanxi Normal University, Taiyuan, 030031, People’s Republic of China

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Sensitive and on-site detection of tannic acid (TA) was important for protecting human health. In this work, Co-doped N-rich carbon dots (Co-N-CDs) were synthesized using the vitamin B12, anhydrous citric acid and ethylenediamine as the precursors. The Co-N-CDs possessed high quantum yield and excellent peroxidase-like activity. The Michaelis constant (Km) of Co-N-CDs using H2O2 as substrate was lower 18.8 times than that of horseradish peroxidase (HRP). Compared to Co-doped carbon dots without ethylenediamine, the maximum reaction rate (Vmax) of Co-N-CDs toward 3,3′,5,5′-tetramethylbenzidine (TMB) was greatly enhanced around 5 times. In addition, the Co-N-CDs could oxidize TMB to oxidative product (oxTMB) in the presence of H2O2. The fluorescence intensity decreased due to the fluorescence resonance energy transfer (FRET) between the oxTMB and Co-N-CDs. When adding TA, the produced oxTMB was reduced, resulting in the absorbance decrease and fluorescence recovery. Based on the principle, a colorimetric and fluorometric dual-mode sensor based on Co-N-CDs nanozyme was constructed to detect TA. The limit of detection for fluorimetry and colorimetry were 0.028 μM and 0.041 μM, respectively. Meanwhile, a smartphone-assisted dual-mode sensing method was constructed to accomplish the portable and visual determination of TA in real samples with satisfactory recoveries, suggesting significant potential in food safety and environmental protection.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: As a natural and water-soluble polyphenolic compound, tannic acid (TA) is widely founded in vegetables and fruits. Due to its structure with polyphenol groups, TA possess unique chemical and biological activities, including high interaction with proteins and metal lions [1], and excellent antioxidation ability. Therefore, TA is widely applied in the fields of medicine, food, metallurgy, printing and dyeing, leather making and so on [2]. For instance, TA is used in beverages and wine as clarifying agent, and in medical fields as a clinical additive [3]. Although TA has a beneficial effect on the human body when intake dosage within the safe limits, the overuse of TA can cause an unpleasant flavor and exert adverse impacts on the human health [4]. Moreover, it is one of the environmental contaminants and has irreversible effects on the environment and ecology [5]. Therefore, there is an urgent need to construct a sensitive, accurate and portable monitoring sensing platform for TA detection.

Currently, many methods have been used to detect TA, including fluorimetry [6–8], electrochemistry [9–11], chemiluminescence [12], chromatography [13] and colorimetry [14]. Among them, some fluorescent or colorimetric sensors have been paid great attention due to their low cost and simple operation, which have been successfully used for TA sensing [15]. For example, Liu et al. designed a fluorometric sensor for the quantification of TA using MoO3-x quantum dots [16]. Wu et al. established a colorimetric sensor based on Fe-N-C MFs nanozymes and was utilized to detect TA [17]. Nevertheless, most of the present reports mainly rely on single absorbance or fluorescent detection that are highly vulnerable to background and environmental changes. Dual-mode sensor provides a built-in self-calibration function and makes the experimental results more accurate and reliable. Therefore, it is highly urgent to develop multi-mode sensing technology.

It is well known that natural enzymes are sensitive to environmental conditions. Nanozymes possess the properties of high stability, easy large-scale preparation and adjustable catalytic activity [18], which are expected to replace natural enzymes [19]. Currently, many nanomaterials have already been used to mimic nanozymes with different enzyme-like activities, such as carbon quantum dots [20], single-atom nanozymes [21,22], noble metal nanoparticles [23–26] and metal-organic frameworks [27]. Carbon dots, as one of the most promising nanomaterials, have gained extensive attention because of their inexpensive cost, simple preparation, low cytotoxicity, and good biocompatibility. So far, carbon dots with enzymatic activity have been applied to determine analytes in the environment and food, such as ascorbic acid [28], H2O2, glucose [29] and ampicillin [30]. In recent years, smartphone has drawn wide attention as a sensing platform for point-of-care testing (POCT) due to its portability and simple operation. Up to now, multiple colorimetric and fluorometric sensors based on smartphones have been reported [31,32]. However, a smartphone-assisted portable sensing platform for TA detection based on carbon dots nanozyme, by combining the colorimetric signal with fluorescence detection analysis, has rarely been reported.

Herein, we proposed a fluorometric and colorimetric method for TA sensing using cobalt-doped carbon dots (Co-N-CDs) nanozyme. The Co-N-CDs were prepared via straightforward hydrothermal process, and showed excellent peroxide-like activity and blue fluorescence emission. The obtained Co-N-CDs can oxidize the colorless 3,3′,5,5′-tetramethylbenzidine (TMB) to oxidative product (oxTMB) with the addition of H2O2 and exhibited UV-Vis absorption peaks at 370 nm and 652 nm. Simultaneously, the generated oxTMB could quench the fluorescence of Co-N-CDs owing to the fluorescence resonance energy transfer (FRET). After adding TA, the oxTMB was reduced, accompanied by a recovery of the fluorescence and a decrease of the absorbance. Moreover, the smartphone-assisted sensor was designed and successfully used to detect TA in real samples ( Scheme 1). In a word, this research aims to provide a novel method for TA detection and shows strong potential applications in food and environmental protection fields.
The detailed information of reagents and apparatus were described in the Supporting Information.

Co-N-CDs were obtained based on the previous study with some modifications [33]. 2 mmol anhydrous citric acid and 0.25 mmol vitamin B12 ultrasonically dissolved in 15 mL ultrapure water, and 150 μL of ethylenediamine was added the above solution. Thereafter, the mixture was transferred to a polyethylene reactor and heated at 180 °C for 8 h. Cooled to room temperature, the acquired solution was centrifugated at 10,000 rpm for 10 min. The supernatant was filtered via a 0.22 μm membrane. Finally, the products were dialyzed against ultrapure water with a dialysis bag and dried under vacuum-freeze to obtain the Co-N-CDs powder. Besides, N-CDs and Co-CDs were synthesized by the before-mentioned procedure, except that vitamin B12 and ethylenediamine were not added, respectively.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Peroxidase-mimic catalytic property of the Co-N-CDs
ce:section.ce:para: The peroxidase-mimic activity of Co-N-CDs was calculated via the addition of TMB and H2O2. Specifically, 150 μL of Co-N-CDs (250 μg/mL), 100 μL TMB (20 mM), 150 μL of H2O2 (10 mM) were mixed to 300 μL of 0.2 M HAc-NaAc buffer (pH=4). Afterwards, the aforementioned solution was fixed to 3 mL with ultrapure water. The absorption spectra was recorded after incubation at room temperature for 50 min. The peroxidase-mimic activities of Co-N-CDs with different catalytic temperatures, pH value, volumes of Co-N-CDs (250 μg/mL) and catalytic time were measured.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Measurement of kinetic parameters of Co-N-CDs
ce:section.ce:para:
The kinetic assays of Co-N-CDs were performed with TMB or H2O2 as the substrate. Various concentrations of H2O2 were added to HAc-NaAc buffer (pH=4) including 12.5 μg/mL Co-N-CDs and 0.67 mM TMB. Instead, various concentrations of TMB were added to HAc-NaAc buffer (pH=4) including 12.5 μg/mL Co-N-CDs and 0.5 mM H2O2. Absorbance value of the above solution at 652 nm was then recorded every 30 s. The value of Michaelis constant (Km) and the maximum reaction rate (Vmax) could be measured in light of the following equation (Eq. (1)) [34]:
(1) 1/V = Km/Vmax[S] + 1/Vmax
Herein, V and Vmax correspond to the initial and the maximum reaction velocity, respectively. [S] and Km correspond to the substrate concentration and the Michaelis-Menten constant, respectively.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Colorimetric and fluorescence detection of TA
ce:section.ce:para: 150 μL of Co-N-CDs (250 μg/mL) and 300 μL of HAc-NaAc buffer (pH=4) were added into a 5 mL centrifuge tube. Afterwards, 100 μL TMB (20 mM), different concentrations of TA and 150 μL of H2O2 (10 mM) were mixed into the above mixture and the reaction system was fixed to 3 mL with ultrapure water. The UV-Vis spectrum was recorded after reaction at room temperature for 50 min.
The procedure based on fluorescence signal was performed in accordance with the above method. The fluorescence intensity of reaction solution was measured under 370 nm excitation.

150 μL of Co-N-CDs (250 μg/mL) and 300 μL of HAc-NaAc buffer (pH=4) were added into a cuvette. Afterwards, 100 μL TMB (20 mM), different concentrations of TA and 150 μL of H2O2 (10 mM) were added into the above solution and the ultrapure water was used to volume up the system to 3 mL. The aforementioned solutions were placed in a box and reacted for 50 min. The colorimetric color images were acquired using the smartphone under a LED light. Similarly, fluorescent images were captured using the smartphone-based sensor with a UV light (365 nm). The smartphone sensing platform was displayed in Fig. S1. The RGB values of colorimetric and fluorescent images were extracted using professional color picker app. On the basis of the formula I= 0.299 R + 0.587 G + 0.114B [35], RGB values were transformed into gray value (I) for the fluorescent mode.

Lake water, beer and green tea were used as real samples to assess the practicability and reliability of the fluorometric and colorimetric detection strategies based on Co-N-CDs nanozyme. Lake water was obtained from Yuxiu lake of Shanxi Normal University and filtered via a 0.22 μm membrane. The commercial beer and green tea were bought from a local super-market. Briefly, a certain amount of beer was filtered by a 0.22 μm membrane and diluted for the following assays. Similarly, 1.6 g of green tea was soaked in 0.06 L of boiling water for 10 min. After cooling, the mixture was filtrated via a 0.22 μm membrane and was diluted for further use. The determination of TA was performed according to the method described in Sections 2.5 and 2.6.

Co-N-CDs were synthesized through a simple hydrothermal method using the vitamin B12 (VB12), anhydrous citric acid (CA) and ethylenediamine as the precursors. The morphology of the Co-N-CDs was investigated using the transmission electron microscopy (TEM). As portrayed in Fig. 1A, the Co-N-CDs showed good dispersibility and a regular sphere, and the average diameter was about 2.58 nm. The high-resolution TEM image demonstrated that Co-N-CDs had the lattice structure and the lattice spacing was about 0.21 nm (Fig. 1A). X-ray diffraction (XRD) diagram displayed an obvious peak at 21.5°, suggesting that Co-N-CDs possessed the graphitic structure (Fig. 1B). The surface groups of Co-N-CDs were explored using the Fourier transform infrared (FT-IR). The as-prepared Co-N-CDs showed the characteristic peaks at 3402 and 3200 cm−1, which corresponded to the stretching vibrations of the O-H and N-H groups (Fig. 1C), respectively. The absorption peaks of 1699 and 1397 cm−1 resulted from the stretching vibrations of CO/CN and CC groups, respectively. The characteristic peaks of Co-N-CDs at 1209 and 1159 cm−1 were allocated to the stretching vibrations of C-N and C-O bonds, respectively. In summary, Co-N-CDs contained hydroxyl, amino and carbonyl groups. In addition, the characteristic peak of 618 cm−1 was assigned to the vibration of Co-N, indicating that cobalt ions were successfully doped into carbon dots and provided the active site for the nanozyme [36].

To further confirm the elemental composition of Co-N-CDs, the XPS analysis was carried out. Fig. 1D revealed that both Co-N-CDs and CDs contained C, O and N. In addition, a typical peak at 780.3 eV was attributed to Co 2p in Co-N-CDs, suggesting the successful doping of Co. The C1s XPS spectra comprised three characteristic peaks located at 284.8 eV (C-C/CC), 286.3 eV (C-O/C-N) and 288.58 eV (CO) ( Fig. 2A, Table S1). As displayed in the N1s spectrum (Fig. 2B, Table S1), three peaks were assigned to pyridinic N (399.14 eV), pyrrolic N (400.16 eV) and graphitic N (401.26 eV) [37], respectively. The pyridinic N and pyrrolic N were the main forms (Table S1), which could offer anchor points for individual Co atom [38]. Due to the close binding energies between pyridinic N and Co-N, the peak at 399.14 eV should also include the Co-N bond [39]. The O1s spectrum (Fig. 2C, Table S1) displayed three peaks at 530.86, 531.91, 533.26 eV, which were allocated to C-O-C, CO and C-OH, respectively [40]. The Co 2p XPS spectrum (Fig. 2D, Table S1) showed Co2+ (781.33 and 796.26 eV), Co3+ (779.64 and 794.81 eV) and the satellite peaks (787.58 and 802.04 eV) [41,42]. Besides, the energy dispersive spectrum (EDS) (Fig. S2) displayed the elemental composition of Co-N-CDs. The atomic percentages of Co-N-CDs were 76.80 % (carbon), 22.08 % (oxygen) and 1.12 % (cobalt), respectively. These results further verified that the cobalt was successfully doped into the carbon dots.

To investigate the optical characteristics of Co-N-CDs, UV–vis absorption spectrum was measured. A notable absorption band at 340 nm was allocated to the n-π* transition [43,44], while the weak shoulder peak at 242 nm was ascribed to the π–π* transition ( Fig. 3A) [45]. Clearly, Co-N-CDs aqueous solution displayed obvious blue light under 365 nm excitation (inset in Fig. 3A). As displayed in Fig. 3A and 3B, the optimal emission signal of Co-N-CDs was observed at 440 nm under 340 nm excitation. Fig. 3B showed that the Co-N-CDs exhibited an excitation-dependent property when excited from 290 nm to 370 nm, which was in accordance with most carbon dots [46]. The quantum yield (QY) was 35.1 % using quinine sulphate as a standard. Moreover, the stability of Co-N-CDs was explored. The fluorescence intensity of Co-N-CDs did not show any remarkable decrease under continuous exposure to a xenon lamp within 120 min (Fig. S3A), demonstrating its excellent photostability. As depicted in Fig. S3B, the fluorescence intensity remained almost unchanged when pH value changed from 4 to 11, which suggested that the Co-N-CDs possessed good physiological stability. The fluorescence intensity did not change significantly when the concentration of NaCl ranged from 0.05 to 4 M (Fig. S3C). These results indicated that Co-N-CDs had excellent fluorescence stability in various environmental conditions, which helped to the potential application of Co-N-CDs in sensing field.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Peroxidase-mimic catalytic feature of Co-N-CDs and optimization of reaction conditions
ce:section.ce:para: The peroxidase-mimic catalytic property of Co-N-CDs was investigated by using TMB as the chromogenic agent in the existence of H2O2. The UV–vis absorption spectrum of TMB, Co-N-CDs + H2O2 and Co-N-CDs systems showed no obvious UV–vis absorption peak at 652 nm, indicating that TMB was not oxidized (Fig. S4A). As a contrast, there was an apparent absorption peak at 652 nm when Co-N-CDs, H2O2 and TMB coexist (Fig. S4A). Although the Co-N-CDs + TMB and TMB + H2O2 systems exhibited slight variations in color (Fig. S4A), the absorbance values of them were very low, which had no effect on the catalytic reaction and were negligible. Compared with Co-CDs and N-CDs, Co-N-CDs displayed stronger peroxidase-mimic activity (Fig. S4B), which manifested that the cobalt and ethylenediamine played crucial parts in the reaction process. Therefore, the Co-N-CDs with different amounts of VB12 and ethylenediamine were synthesized. Fig. 4A exhibited that the catalytic activity was enhanced with the increase of VB12 content, while quantum yield using quinine sulphate as a standard gradually reduced. To balance these two properties, 0.25 mmol was chosen for the following measurement. As depicted in Fig. 4B, the peroxidase-like activity was greatly enhanced after introducing ethylenediamine. The catalytic activity was highest when the volume of ethylenediamine was 150 μL. Therefore, 150 μL was used for preparation of Co-N-CDs.

As we all know, enzyme activity was affected by many factors, including enzyme concentration, pH values, reaction temperature and reaction time [47]. Therefore, the aforementioned factors were optimized. The influence of pH values ranging from 2 to 7 was studied. The absorbance intensity at 652 nm increased first and then reduced, reaching the maximum at 4.0 (Fig. S5A). Fig. S5B exhibited that a steady state was reached after reaction for 50 min. Thus, 50 min was chosen as the reaction time for the following measurement. The catalytic activity increased fast when the volumes of Co-N-CDs increased from 50 μL to 210 μL (Fig. S5C). In order to improve the accuracy of experimental results, 150 μL was chosen in the following study. Fig. S5D demonstrated that the catalytic activity was the highest when the temperature was 25 °C.

In order to further assess and compare the peroxidase-mimic capability of Co-N-CDs and Co-CDs, the steady-state kinetic assays based on TMB or H2O2 as substrates were carried out under optimal conditions. As depicted in Fig. 5 and Fig. S6, both H2O2 and TMB were verified to accord with the standard Michaelis-Menten model. According to Lineweaver-Burk plots, the Km and Vmax were calculated (Fig. 5 and Fig. S6). As manifested in Table S2, the Vmax of Co-N-CDs was higher 5 and 3 times toward TMB and H2O2 than that of Co-CDs, respectively, which indicated that the addition of ethylenediamine can significantly enhance the catalytic activity. The Km of Co-N-CDs using H2O2 as substrate was lower 18.8 times than that of horseradish peroxidase (HRP), and the Km of Co-N-CDs toward TMB was also lower than that of HRP, which indicated that the Co-N-CDs had stronger affinity toward TMB and H2O2. In addition, the Vmax of H2O2 and TMB were comparable to that of HRP. Compared to some reported nanozymes, the as-prepared Co-N-CDs showed higher Vmax and smaller Km. In a word, the Co-N-CDs possessed remarkable peroxidase-like characteristic, which contributed to its application in the analysis area.

To explore the mechanism of peroxidase-mimic activity of Co-N-CDs, the type of multi-substrate enzymatic reaction was studied. The Lineweaver-Burk plots were acquired by varying the concentration of H2O2 and TMB. As illustrated in Fig. 6A and 6B, the fitting lines were approximately parallel to each other, revealing that catalytic process of Co-N-CD nanozyme conformed to the ping-pong mechanism. In other words, Co-N-CD nanozyme reacted with the first substrate to form the first product and the generated complex was released. Then, the second substrate combined with the Co-N-CD nanozyme. Therefore, the types of intermediate products were explored.

Firstly, the radical inhibition assays were performed to identify the type of reactive oxygen species (ROS). Isopropanol (IPA), 1, 4-benzoquinone (PBQ), and L-histidine (L-His) were used as scavengers to capture •OH, •O2 − and 1O2, respectively [48]. When IPA was added in the reaction system, the catalytic performance was almost unaffected compared to the blank (Fig. 6C), suggesting that •OH was not produced in the catalytic process. However, the marked declines in the relative activity were seen with the addition of L-His and PBQ, revealing that 1O2 and •O2 − were generated. To further verify the produced ROS, the electron paramagnetic resonance (EPR) experiment was performed. As displayed in Fig. 6D and 6E, Co-N-CDs nanozyme produced 1O2 and •O2 − in the presence of H2O2.

Based on the above results and relevant reports [49,50], we concluded that the peroxidase-like catalytic mechanism was ascribed to Fenton-like reaction and the electron transfer process. In detail, the Co-N-CDs nanozyme can catalyst H2O2 to generate 1O2 and •O2 −, and oxidized the TMB to oxTMB with two absorption peaks at 370 nm and 652 nm (Fig. 6F). When adding TA, the blue oxTMB was reduced to TMB, and the absorbance value at 652 nm decreased. On basis of the above analysis, a sensing platform can be designed to determine TA for colorimetric mode.

To investigate the principle of fluorescence sensor, the emission and excitation spectrums of Co-N-CDs and the absorption spectrum of oxidative oxTMB were recorded. As described in Fig. 7A , the UV-Vis absorption spectrum of oxTMB overlapped with both the excitation spectrum and emission spectrum of Co-N-CDs. This result suggested that fluorescence quenching probably was caused by inner filter effect (IFE) or FRET. Fluorescence lifetimes of Co-N-CDs were detected to further elucidate the quenching mechanism before and after adding the oxTMB. Fig. 7B showed that the fluorescence lifetime decreased from 14.91 ns to 13.01 ns with the addition of oxTMB, which manifested that oxTMB-induced fluorescence quenching was mainly attributed to FRET.

A colorimetric sensor based on Co-N-CDs nanozyme was constructed to quantify TA. As portrayed in Fig. 8A, the absorbance values at 652 nm gradually decreased as the concentrations of TA increased. Fig. 8B displayed that the linear correlation between TA concentration and change of absorbance ΔA (ΔA= A0-A) was expressed by the equation y = 0.0025 + 0.0748 x with R2= 0.9948. The linear range of colorimetric mode was 0.041–8 µM with detection limit of 0.041 μM.

Meanwhile, a sensitive fluorescence probe was constructed to detect TA. To improve sensitivity of sensor, excitation wavelength was optimized when adding TA into the reaction system. The fluorescence intensity ratio F/F0 was highest with excitation of 370 nm (Fig. S7). Thus, 370 nm and 450 nm were chosen as excitation and emission wavelength for following measurement, respectively. As depicted in Fig. 8C, as the concentration of TA increased, the fluorescence intensity of Co-N-CDs was gradually increased. Fig. 8D displayed that the F/F0 and TA concentration showed an outstanding linear relationship in the concentration range of 0.028–8 μM. The LOD was calculated to be 0.028 μM. The constructed sensor had lower detection limits for TA detection compared with other reports (Table S3).

To assess the practicality of dual-signal model, selective and anti-interference experiments were performed. As displayed in Fig. 9A and 9B, the interfering substances including citric acid, tartaric acid, oxalic acid, glucose, sucrose, L-Arg, lysine, K+, Na+, Zn2+, Mg2+, Al3+, SO4 2-,Cl-, CO3 2- had negligible impact on the determination of TA for the fluorescence and colorimetric dual-mode sensor, even though their concentrations were 3 times higher than that of TA. In addition, ascorbic acid, as a powerful antioxidant, showed fewer effect on reaction system compared with TA. Unfortunately, gallic acid (GA) in the same concentration as TA had certain interfering effect on TA detection. However, concentrations of GA in real samples such as beer [51,52] and tea [53,54] were very low compared to TA. Therefore, the influence of GA on TA detection in actual samples was negligible. Those results demonstrated that the dual-mode sensing platform had remarkable selectivity in the detection of TA. In addition, Co-N-CDs exhibited excellent catalytic property during storage for one month (Fig. 9C).

To evaluate the reliability of dual-mode sensing method based on Co-N-CDs nanozyme, three different samples including lake water, green tea and beer were used to determine the amount of TA. Different concentrations of TA were added to the samples. Table 1 indicated that the spiked recoveries of colorimetric and fluorometric method were 94.9 %-108.3 % and 97 %-106.3 %, respectively. Simultaneously, the RSD of all test results were less than 6 %. As listed in Table S4, the above results were consistent with those of high performance liquid chromatography (HPLC). These results showed that the dual-signal sensor had the potential to quantify TA in real sample analysis.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Smartphone-assisted dual-mode sensor for TA detection in actual samples
ce:section.ce:para: To realize on-site and portable determination of TA, a smartphone-assisted dual-mode sensing platform was established. As displayed in Fig. 10A, 10B and 10C, different concentrations of TA were first mixed into the solution, including TMB, H2O2 and Co-N-CDs. After 50 min, the smartphone was used to acquire a series of concentration-dependent colorimetric and fluorescent color images and RGB values were extracted using color picker app. Fig. 10D showed that there was a good linear relationship of R/B versus the concentration of TA ranging from 0.151 μM to 8 μM for colorimetric mode. The limit of detection was calculated to 0.151 μM. Meanwhile, an obvious color change was observed with the increase of TA concentration by the naked eye (Fig. 10B). Similarly, the fluorescent images were analyzed and converted into RGB values via color picker app. It can be seen that a good linear correlation of I/I0 versus TA in the range of 0.125–8 μM was acquired (Fig. 10E). The linear correlation was expressed by the equation y = 1.0331 + 0.1501 x and the detection limit was estimated to be 0.125 μM.

In order to assess the practicability of smartphone-assisted portable sensor, selective and anti-interference experiments were carried out. As described in Fig. S8, these interfering substances did not interfere with the determination of TA. Besides, the sensing platform was utilized to quantify TA in lake, beer and green tea. The spiked recoveries of colorimetric method were 98.3 %-102 % and the RSD was less than 3 % (Table S5). The spiked recoveries were 92.2 %-105 % and the RSD was less than 6 % for the fluorescent mode. The above results revealed that the smartphone-assisted sensor can quickly and accurately test TA.

To sum up, a dual-mode sensor based on Co-N-CDs nanozyme was established to detect TA. The Co-N-CDs showed excellent peroxidase-like activity and photometric characteristics. The Vmax of Co-N-CDs was greatly enhanced with the introduction of ethylenediamine. The catalytic process of Co-N-CDs nanozyme conformed to the ping-pong mechanism. Besides, the dual-mode sensor displayed wide linear range and low detection limits. More importantly, the smartphone-assisted dual-mode sensor was constructed and realized the on-site and efficient detection of TA in real samples, and offered a hopeful perspective for protecting human health.
This research did not involve human or animal samples.
This research did not receive any specific grant from funding agencies in the public, commercial, or not-for-profit sectors.

Xiaoqing Song: Writing – original draft, Investigation, Formal analysis. Xiaoye Wen: Methodology, Formal analysis, Conceptualization. Yongfei Huang: Investigation, Data curation. Zhefeng Fan: Writing – review & editing, Formal analysis.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data associated with this article can be found in the online version at doi:10.1016/j.colsurfa.2025.136484.
